meta.title: Fireside: Galaxy's Mike Novogratz and Helius' Mert Mumtaz

transcript:
Mert Mumtaz: It's a good crowd.

Mike Novogratz: Yes it is.

Mert Mumtaz: All right. Hello everybody. Uh, hello. I am Mert, the CEO of Helius, and I'm here with Mike, my fellow haircut brother.

Mike Novogratz: Yes, true.

Mert Mumtaz: Um, and today we're going to talk about a few things: Solana, Galaxy, markets, and everything else. So Mike, let's start with your big news of the day, came out a few hours ago. So it seems Galaxy is launching this thing called Sweep, a tokenized liquidity fund, uh, and it's on Solana. So tell me about exactly what Sweep is, how it works, why it's important.

Mike Novogratz: Yeah, so Sweep is a... is State Street's, you know, $5 trillion asset manager, uh, version of BlackRock's BUIDL. It's a tokenized money market fund. Uh, with some key differences. Uh, the biggest difference is instantly when you redeem, it gives you PayPal's stablecoin, PYUSD. So if you think about what should happen in the world, right, you should have interest-bearing cash. That's, in the US right now, illegal, right? The stablecoin bill is, uh, said not... not happening. But if you have a money market fund that redeems into a... into a stablecoin, it's roughly the same thing. And so I envision a world where people, not necessarily in the United States, but broadly around the world, are carrying, you know, US Treasury stable... uh, money market fund, Sweep, and when they want to spend it, they flip it into a stablecoin and spend it. And so instead of carrying cash in your wallet that gives you no interest, you're getting paid, uh, on everything you own. And... and that is the trend in crypto.

Mert Mumtaz: Yes. And so that's exciting. And it... today it is only on Solana. And you guys are actually quite, uh, let's say... you... you're big fans of Solana. You have also tokenized your stock, right? You guys went public.

Mike Novogratz: We were the first tokenized equity, uh, that the SEC approved. There's lots of forms of tokenized equities, there are xShares and some different, uh, exchanges, but working with the SEC in the US to get a, you know, SEC-sanctioned tokenized equity is what we did first. It trades on a thing called Superstate, which quite frankly is a walled garden right now. So it's not... as exciting that we did it, it's not that exciting for liquidity because it's trade by appointment in some ways. What will happen soon, and this is the real exciting, is that the SEC in the US has taken a complete 180-degree turn from the old SEC. The era of Gary Gensler ended. Uh, this new administration says we want everything to move on-chain. I was like, excuse me? We want everything to move on-chain. And I'm not even sure they knew what they said when they said it, but they're heading that direction. It is a radical, radical departure. It is awesome for, uh, this industry. Um, and so we're hoping soon, and we're in discussions all the time—I was actually with Paul Atkins two weeks ago—uh, is to get a, uh, what do they call the... the... like the sandbox exemption, to actually try to see, you know, our stock, our tokenized stock start to trade on decentralized platforms and on... And that I think will be a radical shift. What's going to happen with tokenization, like there's a lot of talk about tokenization right now. There's two pieces to it. There's one piece which is efficiency. Yada yada yada. That's... it's very important, right? There's going to be for collateral management, for settlement, for all these things, you're going to tokenize equities. But the bigger piece really is for access. And it's mostly access in the rest of the world. I love coming to this place because I can draw a... I can put a pin in Abu Dhabi and then do a 500-mile circle and you see what it encompasses, right? The... there's a tremendous amount of population in South Asia, in Africa, in the Mid East. Uh, it's those populations that are going to benefit most from both stablecoins and tokenization. You're going to have the young kid in Paraguay who wants to buy some Apple stock or some SpaceX token, um, that never had access to it. And so you're going to see companies like Galaxy, hopefully, be the banker, the conduit between these brands that you've heard of, big US capitalized companies, big US, um, credit businesses or, you know, credit products, tokenize it and have it distributed to the 6 billion, 7 billion people in the rest of the world.

Mert Mumtaz: Right. Yes. And uh...

Mike Novogratz: And that's really crypto's best use case. It's unbanking, it's giving financial services to people that didn't have access.

Mert Mumtaz: Yes, increasing access to financial markets, international capital markets. And yeah, I... I agree that Gensler is an abomination to, uh, baldness. Um, I... I would say, okay, so one thing that's interesting here is there's a lot of other chains and you're... you're... you've been around, you're a pro. You guys are very strong on Solana. You have a massive validator. You're collaborating with, uh, Jump and Multicoin on the... that... um, you're doing this fund. How are you thinking about the state of different blockchains? You know, everybody's talking about, okay, L1s overvalued, you know, are... do they have no moat? Is Stripe just going to come in and take everything? How... how is your mental map on...

Mike Novogratz: So this is a great, great question. So we're in a really weird... not weird, an interesting transition in crypto, right? For the first chapter, the story broadly was: this is a really important industry, it's going to change everything. Pretty easy narrative. Everyone could repeat it. I could make a choir of it. Right? That was the story for the guys that got on stages. Now... but you couldn't really build so much on top of it that was scalable because blockchains weren't fast enough, blockchains weren't trusted enough, they weren't scalable enough, and there was no legislation, there was no regulation, certainly in the United States, that allowed companies to feel comfortable. Right? You build something, you build a token that makes sense, and you might end up with a Wells Notice or getting, you know, sued or worse than that, arrested. And so we had this stagnation period where the only thing you could do was tell that story about how important we are. And crypto was mostly used, outside of Bitcoin as a store of value and stablecoins, as an experiment and a... and a... and a token gambling platform. Right? So we had big exchanges like Binance and Bybit and, you know, Arthur Hayes originally BitMEX, providing lots of leverage which gave people a chance to speculate. We're now at the era where crypto has to work, where crypto has to make a difference to normal people's lives. And we have fast enough blockchains and we have a regulatory environment that is coming in the United States. And I know I sound American-centric because I'm a Yankee. Uh, the US is 65% of global market cap in equities. Right? You need the US regulatory framework for an industry to really be global. Um, it... it just has too much power. It might not in the future, but right now it does. And so the good news is it's coming our way. Both Republicans, Democrats, and more importantly the regulators are all starting to see it the same way. Uh, and so we're going to get good crypto legislation and better than... more important, good crypto regulation. The tough news for the people in this industry is it's time to build shit that people use. And we're starting to see it. And so how... how is this blockchain war going to work out? So blockchains are valued two ways. Crypto is valued two ways. The most important valuation metric in crypto is community. Right? People ask me all the time, how can XRP be the third most valuable crypto? Like people don't use their chain that often. Uh, they have an unbelievably tight community and passionate community. Right? All of these chains at one level are multi-level marketing schemes. You got to bring people in the community, you got to tell the story. And so conferences like this help Solana tell its story. It's hard for new chains to get enough air to tell the story because most chains do the same thing. They provide blockspace. Right? Blockchains are basically giant databases that are decentralized. Right? Think about an Excel spreadsheet. We're all selling some version of the Excel spreadsheet, uh, with different features. And so I do think community is going to continue to remain important, but to have a narrative that the community believes, you're going to need to see real shit happen. You're going to need to see growth. The value of these chains will not be some fractional discounted value of future cash flows. Right? That's how we used to value everything. It's going to be partly that and a lot of community. And so I think you'll see a few chains continue to thrive. We love Solana because it's so damn fast. Right? It was tailor-built to be the capital markets chain. So why Jump Capital became our partner in the... that is they are a high-frequency trading shop and one of the best in the world. I did that deal because I wanted to be close to Jump because I was like, those guys are so good at what they do of building infrastructure, I want to see what I can wean off of them. And so I think Solana's future is bright. I do. But for it to really be bright, it has to win. It has to be a chain that people build cool innovative products on. And that's what we're trying to do with tokenizing our stock, with tokenizing a money market fund. We got a couple other things coming down the road. And if more and more people use it, great. Listen, in the long run, most things will be compatible. There will be bridges. We will have this... if you think about in the United States you have a bunch of states and you have the Interstate Highway Commission... the Interstate Highway Commission holds the state... you know, the highways together. You will have blockchains and bridges that hold them together. Uh, and so I don't think there'll be one chain or two chains. There'll be a patchwork of chains. But if you want value in your chain, you got to tell the story of the chain and the story has got to be backed up by shit that matters.

Mert Mumtaz: Shit that matters. Excellent. Okay. Well, uh, that was a great answer. Final question is going to be a selfish one from me. So the... the price keeps going up and then keeps going down and then keeps going up and then I... I don't... I can't quite understand what's happening. And you're obviously an expert in macro. How... how do you make sense of what's happening in global markets right now?

Mike Novogratz: Yeah. Um, so if you had told people at the beginning of the year that stocks were going to be on their highs, the Fed was going to be cutting, we were about to get a new Fed governor who was going to feel a little bit more like a Turkish central banker than a US central banker, i.e. who's going to maybe do the bidding of the president, uh, you would have never thought that Bitcoin was at 90,000 and... and Solana was at 140. Um, you would have been much more optimistic. And, you know, crypto prices this year have been painful. So what happened? I think a couple things happened. I'll start with Bitcoin and I know I'm going to take now an extra minute and they might like run the music and get a hook and pull me off, but damn it, I came all the way from New York for this talk. Um, what happened? First, Bitcoin hit 100,000 and it felt like we won the marathon. For all the people that bought it at a dollar or $10 or $100 or $1,000 or $6,000, it was a huge victory. And I remember the feeling. We put out a video congratulating the community on that great accomplishment. I actually think that sense of "we did it" triggered something in a few big owners to say, it's time to take a couple chips off the table. I've literally struggled and survived up and down for 13 years in this damn token. I'm now going to take some chips off the table. And so that became a little bit of a virus. Right? Galaxy alone had one client that sold $9 billion of Bitcoin. Uh, 9 billion. That's one-third of everything that, you know, BlackRock bought this year. Uh, and so I do think we're going through a period of old people selling some of their crypto to diversify their portfolio and new people buying it. And the new people are mostly US wealth. Right? US wealth is $45 trillion. When I say US wealth, money that is held by registered investment advisors that broadly is owned by baby boomers in America, they are starting to buy. It's a whole lot of money. And so it's that transition that's happening. In the non-Bitcoin space, in Solana and the rest, people are starting to say, okay, now that Novogratz and other people are saying it's time for it to be used and the regulators are allowing it to be used, well how does it work? And when we got away from the story that "we're just important and this is what's going to happen" to "let me explain how stablecoins process on top of blockchains," the story got a little more unclear. You also had chains like Hyperliquid come out where the token economics are really clear. They take the profits of what they make each day in the exchange and they buy back tokens. So it feels more equity-like. So lots of investors can say, hey, I can get my arms around that, as opposed to this more effusive idea of community. Right? What... what does community mean even? Uh, and so we're in this transition. And I think prices are going to go down and up. I was more bullish... I've been here in Abu Dhabi for a few days and I'm leaving more bullish than I came. And it's because at the... you know, Abu Dhabi... Abu Dhabi Finance... Abu Dhabi Finance Week, they literally had a world... world-class group of investors from this region but from around the world. And talking to lots of the kings of the industries, I am sure the movement into a future of blockchains and digital assets is accelerating, not decelerating. There is zero doubt in my mind that digital assets are going to be a far more important part of the world in two years and four years and six years. And so that will give our industry fuel. That doesn't mean in the short run selling pressure, buying pressure is going to hit its equilibrium. And so I think it's more challenging today for me to make a three-month or a three-week forecast than normally. Um, right? I see flows on both sides. It's less challenging for me to tell a young kid who's coming into this industry, dude, you're going to have a really cool job in four years. Right? Eight years ago when kids came into the industry I was like, I think this is going to work? But you might spend four years and be shit out of luck. And... and I don't feel that anymore. But that's the... that's the disconnect we have right now.

Mert Mumtaz: Cool. Okay. So you're optimistic, bullish. Excellent. Well, thank you so much for the time. Thanks for listening.

Mike Novogratz: Guys, I... I owe you all four minutes.

tags.impact_reason: Galaxy launching a tokenized money market fund (Sweep) on Solana with PYUSD redemption is a major move that could bring significant institutional interest and liquidity to the Solana ecosystem.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a massive, architectural 'sweeper' funnel as the Semantic Anchor to directly visualize the product's name ('Sweep') and its core function: funneling assets from an interest-bearing fund into a spendable stablecoin. The vast, institutional materials like brushed aluminum and sterile concrete represent the gravitas of State Street and the institutional tag. I included the text '5%' as a diegetic holographic projection on the mechanism to highlight the key user benefit emphasized in the transcript—earning a significant yield on otherwise idle on-chain cash.
visual_idea.description: A monumental, brushed aluminum funnel mechanism is built into a vast, minimalist concrete wall, reminiscent of a futuristic bank vault. A torrent of glowing, emerald-green digital particles, representing the Solana-based fund, pours into the top of the funnel. From the side of the monolithic structure, a large, stable holographic projection displays '5%', integrated as a piece of diagnostic data. In the background, through atmospheric haze and shafts of volumetric light, we see the distant glow of server farm activity, adding a sense of immense depth and scale. The entire scene is clean, sterile, and powerful.

final_prompt.prompt_text: Vertical composition, emphasizing immense height and monumental scale. A monumental, brushed aluminum funnel mechanism is built into a vast, minimalist concrete wall, reminiscent of a futuristic bank vault. The towering concrete wall and funnel dominate the lower and mid-frame, with significant negative space at the top to convey epic vastness. The perspective is low-angle, looking up at the colossal structure. A torrent of glowing, emerald-green digital particles, representing the Solana-based fund, pours into the top of the funnel. From the side of the monolithic structure, a large, stable holographic projection, shimmering with internal light and a slight digital glow, displays the numerical characters '5%', integrated as a piece of diagnostic data. In the background, through atmospheric haze and dramatic shafts of volumetric light, we see the distant, ethereal glow of server farm activity, adding a sense of immense depth and scale. The entire scene is clean, sterile, and powerfully imposing, with sharp architectural lines, polished metallic surfaces, and subtle reflections emphasizing texture. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), ultra-photorealistic, high dynamic range, cinematic lighting, dramatic shadows and highlights, subtle depth-of-field.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a massive, architectural 'sweeper' funnel as the Semantic Anchor to directly visualize the product's name ('Sweep') and its core function: funneling assets from an interest-bearing fund into a spendable stablecoin. The vast, institutional materials like brushed aluminum and sterile concrete represent the gravitas of State Street and the institutional tag. visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the point of transformation within the 'Sweep' mechanism. We see the chaotic, swirling mass of glowing green fund particles being compressed and organized by the brushed aluminum interior of the funnel. The particles begin to coalesce, their ethereal light solidifying into the recognizable, crisp form of a physical coin emblazoned with the PYUSD logo. The focus is on the detailed textures of metal versus the energy of the data stream.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A perfectly symmetrical, centrally focused macro shot, filling the 1:1 square frame. The scene captures the precise point of transformation within a 'Sweep' mechanism, rendered with a strong Institutional Futurism aesthetic. A chaotic, swirling mass of vibrant glowing green holographic fund particles converges dramatically within the finely brushed aluminum interior of a funnel, emphasizing its detailed texture. Volumetric lighting emanates from the particles, creating ethereal light shafts and highlighting the precise, cool brushed aluminum. The particles are actively compressed and organized, their ethereal light intensely solidifying into the crisp, recognizable form of a physical coin. This newly formed coin, crafted from a lustrous metallic material, is emblazoned with the 'PYUSD' logo, deeply engraved or intricately embossed into its surface, reflecting the surrounding volumetric light. The primary focus is on the intricate, contrasting textures: the precise brushed aluminum of the funnel and the dynamic, energetic glow of the holographic data stream particles and the newly formed coin. The entire composition highlights the detailed interplay between solid, engineered materials and ethereal, transforming energy. 8k, octane render, raytracing, physically based rendering (PBR), cinematic lighting, high dynamic range, hyper-detailed, studio product photography, global illumination.

editorial.headline: State Street and Galaxy Launch Tokenized "Sweep" Fund on Solana
editorial.standfirst: The $5 trillion asset manager enters the ecosystem with a money market fund that redeems instantly into PayPal’s PYUSD. Mike Novogratz frames the move as a regulatory workaround to deliver interest-bearing on-chain cash.
key_takeaways: Galaxy and State Street launch "Sweep," a tokenized money market fund on Solana.
The product features instant redemption into PayPal's PYUSD, acting as a proxy for interest-bearing stablecoins.
Novogratz reveals a single client offloaded $9 billion in Bitcoin recently, driving market volatility.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
In a move to rival BlackRock’s BUIDL, **Galaxy** has partnered with **State Street** to launch "Sweep," a tokenized money market fund on **Solana**. The product is designed to solve one of the most persistent inefficiencies in the digital asset market: the lack of yield on idle stablecoins due to U.S. regulatory constraints. By structuring the product as a money market fund that instantly redeems into **PayPal’s PYUSD**, the firms are effectively creating an interest-bearing cash equivalent that remains compliant with current securities laws. Novogratz positioned this as a tool for global "unbanking," targeting users in regions like South Asia and Africa who require access to U.S. Treasury yields without traditional banking friction.

The launch coincides with what **Mike Novogratz** describes as a "radical 180-degree turn" by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Following the exit of Gary Gensler, the new administration has reportedly signaled a desire to move financial assets on-chain. **Galaxy** is currently in discussions to secure a "sandbox exemption" that would allow their tokenized equity—currently trapped in the "walled garden" of **Superstate**—to trade on decentralized platforms. This regulatory pivot marks a transition from the industry's "narrative phase" to a "utility phase," where infrastructure must support real-world volume rather than just speculative storytelling.

Despite the institutional progress, Novogratz offered a sober assessment of recent market volatility. He revealed that **Galaxy** facilitated the sale of $9 billion in Bitcoin for a single client following the asset's run to $100,000. This massive profit-taking event by early adopters ("old people") is currently being absorbed by new entrants, primarily U.S. wealth managed by Registered Investment Advisors (RIAs). Novogratz argues that while this capital rotation creates short-term price chop, the long-term trajectory is secured by the entry of giants like **State Street** and the technical readiness of high-throughput chains like **Solana**.
